CMI's IBD microbiome work + full-length 16S species resolution

Dr. Bartko,
 
I enjoyed your team's recent mSystems paper on diet-microbiome patterns across populations—the culturally-specific bacterial associations align perfectly with precision medicine goals. Your corresponding authorship signals CMI's strategic pivot toward personalized interventions, which is why I'm reaching out.
 
At DNA ME, we've developed a hybrid workflow combining Oxford Nanopore full-length 16S sequencing (V1-V9, ~1,500 bp) with targeted hybridization enrichment. This solves three bottlenecks I've observed in CMI's IBD research pipeline: (1) species-level resolution—moving beyond genus-level taxonomic assignments to differentiate E. coli from Shigella or resolve Bacteroides to species in treatment-response cohorts; (2) clinical throughput—processing 96 samples per run at <$45/sample, making longitudinal monitoring economically viable; (3) CLIA-pathway compatibility—our protocol integrates with FDA LDT frameworks for translational studies.
 
Given CMI's active Crohn's Disease trials (treat-to-target endoscopic remission, maintenance therapy optimization) and your stated goal to "subtype CD patients beyond anatomical classification" using microbiomarkers, I propose a pilot validation study: 500 paired samples (stool + mucosal biopsies) from your IBD cohorts, processed in parallel with your current V3-V4 Illumina workflow over 3-4 months. We'd benchmark species-level taxonomic concordance, functional gene recovery, and correlation with endoscopic inflammation scores.
 
Would you be open to an exploratory call next week to discuss CMI's sample availability and timeline?
 
Kind regards,
 
Evgenii Varlygin
GF / Chief Executive Officer

Pestalozzistrasse 25, DE-22305 Hamburg

Dear Dr. Hess,
 
I read your Thrive Results overview on using precision testing (nutrition genomics, organic acids, and microbiome) to build truly individualized protocols—and I appreciate the emphasis on validating progress with objective biomarkers, not guesswork. Your clinical focus on inflammation/metabolic health (and the reality that keto “isn’t the best diet protocol for everyone”) is exactly where microbiome resolution can turn good coaching into measurable outcomes.
 
DNA ME helps clinicians move from broad “dysbiosis” language to specific, reproducible calls by combining Oxford Nanopore full-length 16S with targeted enrichment. Three advantages that map directly to your practice: (1) Species resolution to separate look-alikes that drive different responses (e.g., beneficial vs. opportunistic species within the same genus); (2) Throughput suited to programs and corporate wellness cohorts, enabling consistent pre/post tracking instead of one-off tests; (3) Clinical workflow readiness—standardized sample-to-report pipelines that can support a future CLIA-aligned pathway as you expand lab-driven offerings.
 
Would you consider a pilot: 500 stool samples over 3–4 months, processed in parallel with your current microbiome vendor so we can benchmark species calls, longitudinal stability, and cost reduction for retesting cadence?

LongTrack + mEnrich-seq meet targeted enrichment: A nanopore synergy?

Dear Dr. Fang,
 
Your recent Nature Microbiology work on LongTrack reshapes strain-level tracking in FMT, especially the 5-year epigenomic stability data showing adaptation signatures—exactly the resolution missing in clinical microbiome diagnostics.
 
I’m reaching out from DNA-ME. We’re developing hybridization-capture workflows optimized for Oxford Nanopore adaptive sampling, enabling targeted enrichment for genomic panels (SNPs/STRs, pharmacogenes, pathogens) at substantially lower cost than whole-genome approaches. We see 2 synergies:
 
Strain tracking at scale: LongTrack yields high-purity strain-level MAGs. Targeted capture could pre-enrich donor/recipient samples prior to assembly, reducing depth while maintaining purity—important at the 648-strain scale you demonstrated.
 
Methylation + probes: mEnrich-seq’s methylation-sensitive background depletion is elegant. Pairing it with probe-based capture could further boost enrichment: deplete host/background first, then capture specific taxa/regions—potentially exceeding the 117-fold gains you reported, especially for low-abundance microbes.
 
We’d like to propose a pilot: ~500 samples over 3–4 months, run in parallel with your existing cohorts to benchmark targeted capture vs standard metagenomics, using LongTrack as the analysis reference. This would be additive only, with no disruption to current workflows.
 
Would you be open to a short call to discuss technical fit and overlap with your Center for Genomic AI and Microbiome Medicine initiatives?
 
Kind regards,
